questions.prompt: Why does feature-first folder structure beat file-type-first at scale?

choices.text: Feature-first (features/auth/, features/posts/, etc., each with components/hooks/api/types inside) keeps related code together — adding a feature is adding a folder; removing is removing a folder. File-type-first (components/, hooks/, utils/ at the root) creates flat lists of 200+ files where related code spreads across folders; finding things requires text search; refactoring a feature touches many folders. Feature-first also enables clean public API per feature (index.ts re-exports the surface; internals stay private). Works for small projects too; essential at scale.

questions.explanation: Feature-first keeps related code together; public API per feature; scales to teams.

questions.prompt: Feature A wants to import a function from Feature B. Where should it import from?

choices.text: From Feature B's public API (its index.ts): `import { fn } from '@/features/b'`. The public API is the contract — Feature A depends on what B explicitly exports. If B's internals refactor (file moves, helper splits, renames), A doesn't break because the public API stays stable. Reaching into another feature's internals (`/features/b/lib/helper`) couples consumers to internal structure; every internal refactor cascades. Enforce via ESLint (`eslint-plugin-boundaries`) for teams: imports across feature boundaries must come from the index.ts.

questions.explanation: Import from the feature's public API (index.ts). Internals can refactor freely.
